mnemonics: Structure for Students, Union for Unification (members share memory)
keyPoints: A structure (struct) is a user-defined data type that groups variables of different types under a single name. Each member has its own memory location.
A union is a user-defined data type that allows storing different data types in the same memory location. Only one member can contain a value at any given time.
Unions are memory efficient but require careful management to know which member is currently holding a valid value.
commonMistakes: Assuming a union's members can all be used simultaneously, leading to corrupted data.
Forgetting that the size of a structure is at least the sum of its members' sizes (plus padding), while a union's size is the size of its largest member.
Accessing a union member that was not the most recently written to, resulting in undefined behavior.
